Al Jazeera: "Most exports from Yanbu [Saudi Arabia] are loaded onto very large crude carriers (VLCCs), but fully laden VLCCs cannot pass through the Suez Canal because they sit too deep in the water. Operators instead would either have to transfer part of the cargo before reaching the canal using Egypt’s SUMED pipeline or switch to smaller Suezmax tankers that can transit the canal more easily. Both options would add time and costs." https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/7/22/can-the-suez-save-asian-oil-consumers-after-houthis-shut-bab-al-mandeb

RT by @no_itsmyturn: Security Alert: U.S. Embassy Jordan July 22 President Trump, Secretary of State Rubio, and the Department of State have no higher priority than the safety and security of American citizens. We remind U.S. citizens that Iran has attacked civilian and critical infrastructure across the Middle East, including hotels, airports, diplomatic facilities, and other civilian locations. Since March 2, the Embassy remains on ordered departure status. Out of an abundance of caution and given continuing regional tensions, the U.S. Embassy in Amman is rescheduling some routine consular-related appointments. U.S. citizens should continue to monitor information from the Jordanian government about specific threats and follow instructions, including sirens and other warnings. Please also monitor all Embassy travel advisories and guidance. Actions to Take: Please visit Jordan’s National Center for Security and Crisis Management website for public safety guidelines, including explanations of emergency sirens in Jordan. Avoid all demonstrations and large gatherings. Avoid all areas where there is a heavy police presence. Monitor local media. Follow the instructions of local authorities. Contact your airline directly for flight change details if your flight is affected. Exercise caution and stay alert at locations publicly associated with the United States. Keep a low profile. Enroll in the Department of State’s Smart Traveler Enrollment Program (STEP). STEP enrollment connects you to the U.S. embassy or consulate so you get the latest security updates. It also makes it easier for the U.S. Embassy or consulate to contact you in an emergency. Assistance: U.S. Embassy – Amman, Jordan Al-Umawyeen 37, Amman, Jordan Phone number (emergencies during business hours): +962 6 590-6000 Phone number (emergencies after business hours): +962 6 590-6500 Email: Amman-ACS@state.gov State Department – Consular Affairs +1 202-501-4444 from abroad 1-888-407-4747 from the U.S./Canada

RT by @no_itsmyturn: The Saudi-US deal on nuclear capabilities is very consequential for a number of reasons. The US reportedly agreed to a deal that falls both below the "gold standard", and doesn't include the Additional Protocol - both of which are made to ensure a civilian program doesn't turn into a military one. This isn't a detail: If Riyadh had wanted to just have US backing for a civilian program, it would have done so years ago - with Washington's blessing. In other words, the goal is to get US support for a deal that doesn't fully exclude a pathway to a nuclear program. That's not to say that there aren't guarantees. But guarantees are purely bilateral, and that means they will be entirely dependent on the state of the US-KSA relation. For Riyadh, this serves a key purpose: To anchor the US in the region, and to make it clear that if the US doesn't come to the Kingdom's rescue, than it has options for self-defense that may include a nuclear program. In a way, this some form of indirect security pact, forcing the US to either commit to Saudi Arabia's security, or potentially face nuclear proliferation. For Israel, this is a clear setback (though we will have to see what happens in Congress). Years ago, such a deal was tied to normalization with Israel. Now that train has passed, despite continued claims by the Israeli government.

RT by @lookner: Dual chokepoint risks deepen Shipping continues through both the Strait of Hormuz and Bab el Mandeb, but operational confidence remains under pressure. Traffic declined sharply across both chokepoints on 21 July, with Strait of Hormuz crossings down 31% day-on-day to nine vessels and Bab el Mandeb down 34% to 29 amid a worsening security backdrop. Four confirmed vessel U turns near the Gulf of Aden suggest operators are becoming more cautious following Houthi threats against Saudi linked shipping. At the same time, confirmed maritime attacks continue around the Strait of Hormuz, reinforcing concerns that uncertainty across both regional chokepoints could reshape routing decisions, increase freight costs and sustain higher geopolitical risk premiums for energy markets. RT by @IntelDoge: This attack came as Ukrainian forces are increasingly short on interceptor missiles which has resulted in a significant increase in the number of missiles getting through and striking their targets.
